title: Childhood Injury Prevention: Age-Appropriate Safety Teaching
subtitle: A curious, newly walking 15-month-old comes in for a well visit. The nurse provides injury-prevention anticipatory guidance — car seats, choking, poisoning, water, burns, and falls — matched to the child's developmental stage.

patient.name: Mia Torres
patient.admitDate: Pediatric clinic for a 15-month well visit. Healthy and thriving; walking, climbing, and mouthing objects. Parents report the home is not yet fully childproofed. Visit focused on injury-prevention anticipatory guidance.

nursesNote: Mia Torres, 15 months, healthy and thriving — walking, climbing, and mouthing objects; home not fully childproofed. Unintentional injury is a leading cause of death in children, and prevention is matched to the developmental stage. Teaching: rear-facing car seat in the back seat; lock medications and chemicals up and out of reach (keep the Poison Control number, do NOT induce vomiting/give ipecac); avoid choking hazards (whole grapes, nuts, popcorn, hot dogs — cut foods small); constant supervision near water (never leave a child alone in the tub/pool); set the water heater to a safe temperature and turn pot handles inward; use stair gates and window guards and lower the crib mattress; store firearms locked and unloaded, separate from ammunition. Childproofing does not replace supervision.
providerOrders: Teach car-seat safety (rear-facing, back seat), safe sleep, and supervision
Teach safe storage of medications/chemicals and choking-hazard avoidance
Provide the Poison Control number; advise against inducing vomiting
Address home hazards (water, burns, falls, firearms); reassess at follow-up

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What should guide childhood safety teaching?

opts.text: Unintentional injury is a leading cause of death in children
Safety teaching should match the child's developmental stage
Infants and toddlers explore by mouthing objects, raising choking risk
Constant supervision is needed near water, even small amounts
Toddlers can be safely left alone briefly in the bathtub
Medications and chemicals should be locked up and out of reach

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What are the key prevention measures?

opts.text: Children should ride rear-facing as long as possible, in the back seat
Choking hazards include whole grapes, nuts, popcorn, and hot dogs
Keep the Poison Control number available; do not induce vomiting or give ipecac
Set the water heater to a safe temperature and turn pot handles inward
A "childproofed" home means supervision is no longer needed
Firearms should be stored locked and unloaded, separate from ammunition
Assess the child's developmental stage and the home environment
Identify the leading injury risks for that age
Teach targeted prevention (car seat, safe storage, supervision, choking)
Provide resources (Poison Control number, car-seat checks)
Reinforce supervision and reassess at future visits; document
Keep infants/toddlers rear-facing in a car seat in the back seat
Store medications and cleaners locked, up and away
Provide constant, close supervision near water
Cut foods small and avoid choking hazards
Use stair gates and window guards; lower the crib mattress
Set the water heater to ≤120°F and turn pot handles inward
Leave a toddler alone in the bathtub "for a minute"
Keep cleaning products under the sink within reach
Give a toddler whole grapes, popcorn, and hard candy

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Review the child's milestones and current safety practices
Teach the highest-priority injury-prevention measures for the age
Address hazards found (medication storage, water, choking foods)
Give the Poison Control number and safety resources
Encourage ongoing supervision and document the teaching
Parents lock away medications and cleaners
The child rides rear-facing in the back seat
Constant supervision near water is maintained
Choking hazards are avoided and foods are cut small
A toddler ingests a cleaning product left under the sink
A child is left alone in the bathtub and nearly drowns
Window guards are absent and a child falls from a window
A loaded, unlocked firearm is accessible in the home

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
